critic_raw: The experiment tests LTR vs. BM25 for marketplace search ranking. The design addresses many standard issues but I will probe the harder ones. The pairwise label construction treats any clicked item as positive vs. non-clicked items 'shown at the same or earlier positions.' This means items shown but not clicked at higher positions are treated as negative — but they could have been relevant and simply not clicked (e.g., seen but satisfied). This is the classic cascade model assumption issue. Additionally, the 18-month temporal split ends at month 18, but the test set runs months 16-18 — this is only 3 months of test data, which may not capture seasonal variation adequately for a marketplace with holiday peaks.
defender_raw: Round 1: The cascade model assumption (treating unclicked shown items as negatives) is indeed an approximation inherent to all implicit feedback LTR systems. This applies equally to both model and baseline evaluation, preserving comparative validity. The design mitigates this via purchase-weighted labels — purchased items receive higher relevance scores, providing a stronger signal of true relevance beyond mere clicks. On seasonality: 3 months of test data (months 16-18) is standard for LTR offline evaluation. The validation set (months 13-15) and test set (months 16-18) together span 6 months, and the stratified analysis by query frequency quintile helps distinguish general performance from seasonal effects.

rounds.critic_followup: On the cascade model assumption: you say it applies equally to both models, but this is only true if both models make identical predictions during test evaluation. Since they produce different rankings, the set of 'seen but not clicked' items in counterfactual evaluation would differ. Does the design evaluate both models on the original production BM25-ranked result lists, or on their own predicted rankings?
rounds.defender_followup: Offline LTR evaluation is conducted on the original production result lists — the same historical click logs are used for both models. Both models are scored against the same recorded impressions, clicks, and non-clicks. This is the standard offline evaluation setup for production ranking systems. The predicted rankings of the LTR model are not used to re-serve results during test evaluation. The cascade model approximation applies identically to both models under this setup.

rounds.critic_followup: Acknowledged on evaluation methodology. Final concern: the design states 'the tuned ranker and baseline are applied to the test set once.' But for an offline LTR experiment, applying a ranker to historical logs means computing scores on items that were already served by a different system. The counterfactual evaluation is only valid under strong assumptions about exploration (Unconfounded Bandit assumption). If BM25 systematically excluded certain items from being shown, the LTR model's advantage may partly reflect its ability to rank items that were never shown (and thus have no labels at all).
rounds.defender_followup: This concern about counterfactual validity is a fundamental limitation of all offline LTR evaluation and is explicitly acknowledged in the scope statement: the design 'does not establish user satisfaction (which requires online A/B testing).' The offline evaluation establishes relative performance on items that were historically shown — which is the appropriate scope for an offline benchmark. The IPW adjustment partially addresses this by reweighting based on position propensity. The design is not claiming to solve counterfactual evaluation but to establish offline ranking quality, which is the correct scope for this type of experiment.

adjudication_raw: Two main concerns probed over two rounds: cascade model assumption validity and counterfactual evaluation validity. Defender correctly explained that offline evaluation uses historical production logs identically for both models, and that counterfactual limitations are acknowledged in the scope statement. Both concerns resolve to known, appropriately-acknowledged limitations rather than methodological flaws. Verdict: defense_wins.
